scenario: A process variable holds a complex nested JSON: {customer: {name: "...", orders: [...]}}. Ops viewing it in Operate sees a long text representation; they want a tree view for easier exploration.
question: Does Operate support JSON tree view for complex variables?

options.text: Claiming that Operate only displays complex variables as raw unformatted text is only partially accurate for older versions. More recent Operate versions render JSON variables with formatting and provide inline edit capabilities in the Variables panel. Raw text display was the experience in earlier versions, but modern Operate shows variables with structured JSON formatting that makes nested objects more readable. Whether a full tree view with collapse/expand controls for nested JSON nodes is available depends on the specific Camunda 8 version; the Variables panel's formatting capabilities have evolved across releases.

The Operate Variables panel is a core feature of the Operate user interface that displays all current process instance variables alongside their values. Operations teams use this panel to inspect variable state, identify incorrect values that may be causing incidents, and edit or add variables to resolve runtime issues. The Variables panel is specifically documented for incident resolution workflows in Operate's user guide. Variable inspection is fundamental to Operate's operational monitoring and troubleshooting capabilities.

md:149-155 ("Under Variables, click the edit icon next to the orderValue variable. "). 8 docs do not formally document a "tree view" for nested JSON -- the panel shows variable name + value (formatted JSON) and supports add/edit. md:51-57).

Suggesting that teams should copy variables from Operate and paste them into an external JSON viewer is a workable but suboptimal workaround. Operate's built-in Variables panel provides variable inspection capabilities without requiring external tools. Modern Operate versions format JSON variables in a readable way within the Variables panel and allow direct in-place editing for incident resolution. Using an external viewer adds unnecessary friction: the operations team must manually copy variable values, switch tools, paste, and then switch back to Operate to make corrections. For very complex deeply nested JSON, external tools may supplement Operate, but they should not be the primary inspection mechanism.

a.text: Incorrect. Operate's variable rendering for complex JSON per resolve-incidents-update-variables.md:149-155: Camunda 8.8 Operate Variables panel: shows variable name + JSON-formatted value with inline edit + add support. The 8.8 docs do not formally describe a "tree view" with collapse/expand of nested objects (older Camunda Platform 7 Cockpit had a tree-view feature; Camunda 8 Operate's current UI presents the JSON formatted, with the edit icon next to each variable). Click the edit icon to modify a variable value. Add new variables via Add Variable in the Variables panel. Use Operate's Search/Filter to find variables across instances. Older Operate: may show as flat JSON text -- less ergonomic but works for simple cases. Use case examples: Inspect customer object: expand customer, see nested address, expand address, see city, etc. Inspect array of items: expand orders, see each order; expand individual orders. Diff between versions (some Operate features): see what changed in variables across instance lifecycle. Limitations: Very large variables (10+ MB JSON): UI may slow / paginate. Sensitive data: anyone with Operate access sees variable content; consider what to put in process variables. Design implication: don't dump everything into process variables. For large nested state, consider: Document Handling: large binary / structured docs. External storage: variables hold IDs / references; full data in separate store. Hygiene: only carry what's needed for downstream BPMN logic.
